study_reported_implication: Nurses were the dominant staff-to-patient transmission route
codeblue_translation: Model nurse-patient contacts as high-priority spread edges

population: 82 participants: 35 patients, 32 nurses/paramedical staff, 15 doctors
hospital_acquired_definition: Confirmed influenza in geriatric short-stay unit
finding_text: Doctor-to-patient transmission rate was lower than nurse-to-patient
study_reported_implication: Doctors transmitted less frequently than nurses
codeblue_translation: Model doctor-patient spread edges as lower-weight than nurse edges

population: 82 participants: 35 patients, 32 nurses/paramedical staff, 15 doctors

finding_text: Nurse-to-nurse transmission rate was estimated at 0.34 per day
study_reported_implication: Staff-staff nurse transmission was relevant

population: 296 hospitalized patients with ILI >=72 h after admission and known vaccination status; 67 confirmed HAI cases
study_design: Incident test-negative case-control study nested in prospective surveillance
hospital_acquired_definition: ILI symptoms >=72 h after hospitalization and positive RT-PCR
comparator_group: HAI cases vs ILI controls with negative RT-PCR
risk_factor_definition: Receipt of one dose of current-season influenza vaccine >14 days before ILI symptom onset
finding_text: Influenza vaccination was independently associated with lower risk of hospital-acquired influenza
clinical_context: Hospitalized patients developing ILI >=72 h after admission
study_reported_implication: Patient vaccination was protective against HAI
codeblue_translation: Add patient influenza vaccination as a strong protective acquisition factor

population: 296 hospitalized patients with ILI >=72 h after admission and known vaccination status; 67 confirmed HAI cases

population: 73,859 adults included after exclusions; 111 HAI cases
hospital_acquired_definition: Positive influenza A or B PCR >4 days after admission
finding_text: Double-room occupancy was the most influential predictor in the random-forest model and HAI was more prevalent in double rooms than in multi-occupancy rooms
key_limitations: Single-center ML study; no per-feature OR reported
study_reported_implication: Room type was the strongest practical predictor of HAI in this model
codeblue_translation: Prioritize room type as a core structural risk input

population: 953 adults hospitalized >7 days; 239 HA influenza cases and 714 controls from the same ward/time window
hospital_acquired_definition: HA influenza diagnosed >=7 days after admission with positive PCR and no evidence at admission

population: Hospitalized short-stay inpatients with influenza-like illness; 24 confirmed HA influenza cases and 141 influenza-negative controls over 2 influenza seasons
study_design: Case-case negative control study nested in a prospective cohort
hospital_acquired_definition: HA influenza defined as virologically confirmed influenza with symptom onset >=72 h after ward admission
comparator_group: Confirmed HA influenza cases vs influenza-negative ILI controls
finding_text: After adjustment, ward healthcare-worker influenza vaccination coverage >=40% was associated with markedly lower risk of hospital-acquired influenza among patients
adjustment_level: Mixed-effect logistic regression adjusted for patient sex, age, potential influenza source on ward in prior 5 days, ward type, and influenza season
clinical_context: Short-stay hospitalized patients with ILI under systematic PCR testing
key_limitations: Pilot multicenter study with limited sample size and only 24 HA cases
study_reported_implication: Higher HCW vaccination coverage appeared protective against HA influenza
codeblue_translation: Add ward HCW vaccination coverage as a protective transmission-context feature for acquisition reasoning
